text.title: WCAG 2.2 New and Changed Success Criteria
text.summary: All 9 new WCAG 2.2 success criteria with levels, requirements, pass/fail examples, and implementation notes; includes the 4.1.1 Parsing removal.

rule.statement: If a help mechanism (help link, contact, chat widget) appears on multiple pages, it must appear in the same relative order on each page.
rule.rationale: Users with cognitive disabilities rely on consistent placement to find help; "consistent" means relative position in the page section, not pixel-exact coordinates.

example.code: Consistent: "Help" link always last item in header nav Consistent: "Contact us" always in footer before legal links Consistent: Chat widget always bottom-right corner
Fail: Help link appears in header on home page, in sidebar on product page Fail: Chat widget visible on landing but hidden on checkout

rule.statement: Do not ask users to enter the same information twice in a session unless security requires re-verification or the previous entry is no longer valid.
rule.rationale: Repeated entry creates barriers for users with cognitive disabilities, motor impairments, and slow typists; also improves conversion for all users.

example.code: Checkout: "Same as billing address" checkbox pre-fills shipping Multi-step form: email entered in step 1 is pre-filled or not asked in step 2
Fail: Step 1 asks for email; Step 2 asks for email again with no justification Fail: Billing and shipping forms both blank with no "copy billing" option

rule.statement: When a component receives keyboard focus, the focus indicator must not be completely hidden by sticky headers, cookie banners, chat widgets, or modal overlays.
rule.rationale: At least a partial focus indicator must remain visible. Fix: add scroll-margin-top matching the sticky header height, or adjust z-index.

rule.statement: Every drag-and-drop operation must have a non-drag alternative (button-driven move or keyboard shortcut); keyboard-only drag is insufficient because screen reader users cannot use it.
rule.rationale: WCAG 2.5.7 (AA). Drag-and-drop completely excludes users with motor disabilities and screen reader users.

example.code: • Item 1

rule.statement: Touch and click targets must be at least 24x24 CSS pixels, or have 24px of spacing around them. WCAG 2.2 AA minimum is 24x24 — not 44x44 (that is the iOS HIG / WCAG 2.5.5 AAA recommendation).
rule.rationale: Agents and older guidance confuse 24px (WCAG 2.2 AA) with 44px (Apple HIG / WCAG AAA). Always cite the correct threshold in code reviews.

rule.statement: Login must not require a cognitive function test — no memorizing passwords character-by-character, no math puzzles. Password managers, biometrics, magic links, and passkeys all pass.
rule.rationale: Must allow paste and autocomplete so password managers work. Never set autocomplete="off" on password fields.

example.code: <!-- Allow password manager autofill --> <input type="email" autocomplete="username"> <input type="password" autocomplete="current-password">
<!-- Fails 3.3.8: blocks paste and password managers --> <input type="password" autocomplete="off" onpaste="return false">

rule.statement: 4.1.1 Parsing has been removed from WCAG 2.2; do not report it as a violation in audits or automated scan output.
rule.rationale: Browsers now handle HTML parsing errors gracefully. Automated tools trained on older data still flag it; strip from outputs. HTML validation remains good practice but is no longer a WCAG criterion.
